nativeRange.habitat: The African Pike Characin inhabits still to slow-moving rivers, floodplains, lakes, and swampy areas across Sub-Saharan Africa. It is typically found near dense marginal or submerged vegetation, which it uses as ambush cover, and generally prefers warm, slightly acidic to neutral water.

decorPreferences: large driftwood and root tangles for cover
dense marginal planting (Vallisneria, Sagittaria) along tank walls

diet.notes: The African Pike Characin is a dedicated ambush hunter that feeds almost exclusively on fish in the wild. Newly acquired specimens typically require live feeder fish to trigger a feeding response; with patient target-feeding using tongs or a feeding stick, some individuals can be transitioned onto large frozen whole fish, frozen smelt, silverside, or fresh fish fillet, though many never accept anything other than live or fresh prey. Dry foods are refused in nearly all cases. Live feeders should be gut-loaded and quarantined before use to reduce the risk of introducing disease; goldfish are not suitable as feeders due to their high fat content and disease risk — guppies, rosy red minnows, or similar small species are better choices. Adults require feeding only two to three times per week; overfeeding accelerates waste buildup and can shorten lifespan.

avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (up to ~40 cm)
other large predatory fish (high aggression risk)
any fish occupying the same swim zone

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Hepsetus odoe. Verify availability and license before use.

summary: The African Pike Characin (Hepsetus odoe) is a large Sub-Saharan ambush predator reaching up to 50 cm, with a torpedo-shaped body and canine-toothed jaws built for hunting fish. It requires a species-only setup of at least 500 litres and a diet of live or large frozen fish.
careNotes:
Taxonomic note: Hepsetus odoe was long treated as a single widespread species across Sub-Saharan Africa, but molecular and morphological research has established that what was called the ‘H. odoe complex’ is in fact several distinct species: H. odoe in the strict sense occurs in West Africa (Senegal to Nigeria), while H. cuvieri is found in the Congo basin, H. lineatus in East Africa, and H. occidentalis in the Upper Niger drainage, among others. Specimens offered in the aquarium trade may belong to any of these species, and reliable identification from photographs alone is generally not possible. Hepsetidae is a small family — currently comprising a single genus with around five described species — and represents a phylogenetically isolated African lineage within the order Characiformes.

The African Pike Characin is a powerful, elongated predator with a laterally compressed body, a large jaw densely armed with canine-like teeth, and well-developed lateral-line scales for detecting water movement from nearby prey. Adults in the wild commonly reach 40–50 cm; captive specimens typically measure 35–45 cm, though individuals approaching 50 cm have been recorded.

Tank requirements: A single adult needs a minimum of 500 litres with a footprint of at least 180 × 60 cm. The species is an explosive burst-swimmer that needs a long, unobstructed mid-water corridor. Dense cover along the tank perimeter — large driftwood tangles and robust rooted plants — provides security and replicates the ambush habitat this fish uses in the wild. Floating plants help diffuse surface glare. Lighting should be subdued, as bright conditions tend to cause chronic stress. A secure, weighted lid is essential: this species is a strong jumper and will exploit any gap.

Water: The African Pike Characin tolerates a range from soft and slightly acidic to moderately hard and neutral water. Keeping the temperature stable in the range of 24–27°C is advisable. Ammonia and nitrite must be maintained at zero; the heavy waste load produced by a large piscivore’s diet demands efficient filtration, and oversized external canister filters or a sump system are strongly recommended. Large, regular water changes — around 30–40% weekly — are generally necessary to manage water quality.

Compatibility: The African Pike Characin is widely considered a species-only fish. Any tankmate small enough to fit in its jaws will be taken, and this species has a deceptively large gape; adults have been documented taking prey nearly half their own body length. Conspecific housing is generally not recommended, as dominance disputes tend to result in serious injury or death. If housing two individuals is attempted in a very large system (1,000 litres or more), multiple large visual barriers are considered necessary.

Availability: This species is rarely encountered in the hobby; specimens are occasionally exported from West or Central Africa as incidental additions to larger fish shipments. Long-term captive success is uncommon, making this a true specialist’s species.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented for home aquaria. In the wild, Hepsetus odoe spawns during the rainy season in flooded marginal vegetation; the female constructs a floating foam nest anchored to plants, and the male guards the eggs. Replicating these conditions in captivity would require a very large aquarium, careful seasonal simulation — including a temperature drop followed by a gradual rise and an increase in water level — and a confirmed mated pair. Sexing individuals outside of breeding condition is difficult, and successful captive breeding in a home aquarium is not considered achievable under ordinary circumstances.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hepsetus odoe
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, adult size, tank requirements, diet, compatibility, and captive care notes. SeriouslyFish provides the most comprehensive hobbyist-oriented account for this species.

additional.name: FishBase — Hepsetus odoe
additional.notes: Cross-referenced for adult size (max 50 cm TL), native range, taxonomy (family Hepsetidae, order Characiformes), temperature range, and habitat description. Also consulted for the species-complex note arising from Decru et al. 2012–2013 revision.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Pike Characin
additional.notes: Used for hobbyist care details including feeding transition strategies, escape risk, and species-only tank recommendation. Supported 500 L minimum tank guidance and noted rarity in the hobby.

additional.name: Decru E. et al. (2012) — Hepsetus species complex revision
additional.notes: Taxonomic reference for the multi-species revision of the H. odoe complex (Decru et al. 2012–2013). Documents H. odoe sensu stricto (West Africa), H. cuvieri (Congo), H. lineatus, and H. occidentalis as distinct species.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Large African Predators in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Supplementary source for captive husbandry context, foam nest breeding behaviour description, and diet/feeding difficulty confirmation in the hobby setting.

fish.breedingNotes: The African Pike Characin is a foam-nest spawner in the wild, constructing nests in flooded marginal vegetation with the male standing guard over the eggs. No successful breeding in a home aquarium has been documented.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches ~50cm; ambush piscivore demanding live or large-frozen feeder fish; requires 500L+ species-only or single-large-fish setup. Rare in trade and rarely successfully kept long-term in home aquaria.
